section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Online platforms' and online services' questionable or unfair practices in regard to

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Identity and account services

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Cryptographic key escrow

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Device activation / device binding / device services

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Dear Sir or Madam,

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: A Choimisineara McGrath, a chara!

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: I write to you in this consultation due to having observed, reasoned about and in some part suffered from harmful practices that endanger the sovereignty and integrity of the EU internal market in regard to digital products and digital services.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: To my understanding the aspects below are not adequately covered by the Digital Markets Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/1925) or Digital Fairness Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/2065) as explained in my text.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: However, the followingly described aspects can very well lead to "financial detriment, time loss, adverse health effects and indirect effects such as environmental costs" of their users. In case of device binding this can render hardware useless, electronic waste and lead to substantial "indirect effects such as environmental costs".

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Virtually all digital services require a login, even the submission to this consultation!

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: From a commercial standpoint, the needed efforts to create a login at a new website or app is a major detriment in the 'onboarding/conversion funnel' to acquire an user.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Thus, we ubiquitously see buttons such as the following, that quickly provide identity and account services (without the else needed efforts to enter personal data and confirm an email-address, instead allowing a only a very shortened, low effort workflow), e.g.:

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: https://developers.facebook.com/docs/facebook-login/

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: https://www.google.com/account/about/sign-in-with-google/

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Page 2

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Log in with PayPal

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: https://developer.paypal.com/docs/log-in-with-paypal/integrate/

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Signin with LinkedIn (Microsoft), https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/linkedin/consumer/integrations/self-serve/sign-in-with-linkedin

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Sign in to LinkedIn with Microsoft ID https://www.linkedin.com/help/linkedin/answer/a6858129

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: This strongly binds the consumer user to a digital platform, in some cases more strongly as the official platform email service (Gmail, Outlook/Hotmail) is the default email used, or the user's real email address is hidden behind a forwarding address of the identity and account services provider, such as Apple here:

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: What is Sign in with Apple? https://support.apple.com/en-mt/102609, as viewed 24.10.2025: "You can use Hide My Email — Apple's private email relay service — to create and share a unique, random email address that forwards to your personal email. This lets you receive useful messages from the app without sharing your personal email address."

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: A more thorough list of such login providers can be viewed e.g. here https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_OAuth_providers (viewed 24.10.2025).

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: This all is even more aggravated by the introduction of Passkeys (https://trusted-digital-identity.europa.eu/eu-login-help/can-i-use-passkey-eu-login_en) which were initially thought to improve the autonomy of the user managing these cryptographic login keys themselves, locally, but more and more appear to be collected/managed/backed up by software and platforms of companies such as Apple or Google, capturing the user to a more or lesser degree.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: As well aggravated to the extreme is that more and more operating systems require an online identity for use, or when resetting a computer (factory reset in combination with activation lock, device binding as explained in the last section), e.g. a Microsoft account required for Microsoft Windows 11 Home, a Google account to login in Google Chrome OS, an Apple ID for a Apple Mac computers' reactivation (after device-binding to an account).

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Problem statement:

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: There can be inadequate options for account recovery for the main account at the identity and account provider.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: To my perception virtually all platforms strongly prefer a fully and only automated account recovery workflow which fails when a) not having setup recovery options, or b) having lost the access to too many initially setup recovery options.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Many users even would be willing to pay a fee for the option of recovery by providing their a government ID, or proofs of having used the service in other ways (email archive, payments for other services of Google/Apple/Microsoft/etc.)

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: DSA/DMA do not provide adequate measures against locking/deletion of accounts: there is e.g. redress for companies whose developer account may have been disallowed / disabled, however not for consumers accessing these app market places, let alone digital services unrelated to the VLOP platforms themselves (especially for identity and account services).

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Proposal:

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: There should be a mandatory recovery option for recovery with a government provided ID, free, or for an adequate fee (< 20 EUR), or other means of proof.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Identity and account services should be defined as a distinct service for which disabling or locking accounts is only allowed under very high standards, a regulatory defined process to object (with oversight by a public body) and at last an adequate timeframe before disabling an account to migrate the plethora of one's 'logins'.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: In some cases - when an account is required to login to a device - the provider should under no circumstances be allowed to disable the account for login at the local device.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Page 3

section_path: Digital Fairness Act: Call for Evidence, Ref. Ares(2025)5829481 - 17/07/2025
paragraphs.text: Page 4
Situation description and problem statement:
The GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) already provides the European consumer with much data sovereignty.
This is not the case when user data does not reside on a digital service provider's servers/cloud, but on the user's own device which is encrypted.
Some encryption needs are warranted and data in any case must remain inaccessible to the user (e.g. banking apps / e-money apps, or in the future the wallet app for a digital Euro)!
Other encryption not only protects, but sometimes bereaves the consumer of their very own personal data (documents, personal memories, communication)!
While in the past this has (only) been the case for mobile devices which are more prone to loss, this becomes more and more pervasive:

Example 1: Windows 10/11 automatically engages drive encryption when the hardware supports it, as soon as a backup of the decryption key has been saved to the OneDrive of the Microsoft ID of the user (cryptographic key escrow).

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/design/device-experiences/oem-bitlocker: "BitLocker automatic device encryption uses BitLocker drive encryption technology to automatically encrypt internal drives after the user completes the Out Of Box Experience (OOBE) on devices that meet hardware requirements."
[note: OOBE includes login with a Microsoft account]
https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/back-up-your-bitlocker-recovery-key-e63607b4-77fb-4ad3-8022-d6dc428fbd0d
"In most situations, your BitLocker recovery key is automatically backed up when BitLocker is first activated:
If you use a Microsoft account, the BitLocker recovery key is typically attached to it, and you can access the recovery key online

If you're using a device that's managed by your work or school, the BitLocker recovery key is typically backed up and managed by your organization's IT department. This is done to ensure that the organization can help you recover your data if your device is not accessible for some reason. It's always a good idea to check with your IT department for the specific procedures and policies related to BitLocker recovery in your organization. It's important to verify that this backup exists and is accessible, or to create an extra backup of your own."

A loss of the access to the Microsoft account which both provides identity and account services and in many cases the only cryptographic key backup, so called cryptographic key escrow can mean forever inaccessible user data stored on the local device.
Both the WhatsApp message database and local backups use mandatory encryption. They are inaccessible to the user outside the WhatsApp application.

"The database that WhatsApp uses to store messages along with other media files can be found [at a certain place in the file system ...] This is a SQLite Database that is encrypted with AES-GCM-256 [...] Under normal conditions on a non-rooted Android device, obtaining this key is not possible because the App-Specific files of any app cannot be accessed by any other applications."
https://snee.la/posts/the-workings-of-whatsapps-end-to-end-encrypted-backups/

It is possible to export individual chats, however - not mentioned in the official FAQ (https://faq.whatsapp.com/1180414079177245/ - the export is restricted to a limited number of messages. The export is not in a structured format, as would be required in a GDPR article 15 request if this data was fully residing in the cloud and not the user's device.

Encryption protects users' chat messages from the access from malicious apps, however, also a) forces users to keep using the WhatsApp app to access their coveted memories, and b) by this creates a vendor lock-in / walled garden, impeding competing messaging apps that - thanks to new EU regulation - can communicate on the WhatsApp network, but not easily migrate / import the existing message history.

Companies that provide identity and account services (as above), which also provide cryptographic key escrow not only for online logins (Passkeys), but for local user data should be subject to even higher standards for account recovery and against disabling user accounts (except for temporary, time-limited) security reasons.

Additionally, users should be informed at time of device setup / app install about encryption & be given the right to export the relevant cryptographic keys of their local device at any time (Note: local key export is possible for Bitlocker, but here the user in my opinion is not adequately informed about the consequences of encryption; there is no known key export to me for WhatsApp).
Consumers (and e.g. even small and medium companies that may only use Windows 11 Home) should be given their right to data sovereignty, especially for data stored on their own devices!
Page 5
Software, interfaces and app market places for smartphones have already very extensively been covered by DSA/DMA and especially competition law.
Interestingly, what has been covered only very lightly, or not at all is

device binding/device activation: Logging in on an Apple device, this by default activates theft protection (per se legitimate), but which is impossible to remove when Apple locks the account illegitimately or not:

Even having access to the exact email and exact phone number of the account that is logged in to the Apple device (existing ‘device-binding’) and knowing the password is insufficient to remove this account, if it has been locked by Apple.

A device bound to such a locked account periodically requests to enter the password (futile, and rather annoying, as even the correct password is not accepted for a locked account), rendering the device difficult to use.

In addition to that it is not possible to install new apps, or install security updates of existing apps after some time (when the technical login token of AppStore has aged too much), as this is not allowed with a locked account and there is no possibility to login with a new account.

In some cases it is possible to have Apple remove the device binding, even when Apple finally refuses to reinstate a locked account forever. However, removal of device binding (or activation lock as commonly referred to)

a) requires a full factory reset (deleting all user data that has not been backed up and requiring a new lengthy setup process), b) has demands many legitimate owners cannot fulfill: Apple requires the user to provide the original invoice dated near the initial device activation (= of the first purchaser). By usual practice, an invoice of purchase on a secondary market place, even from refurbishers of leasing devices (where there typically isn't an invoice for a single device) does not appear sufficient according to many internet complaints.

As the above record has been a collection of personal experience, chats with Apple, information from a large number of help / FAQ entries / forum discussions on the Apple support websites and elsewhere on the internet, I kindly ask you to formally inquire about Apple’s practices and procedures, as well as the smartphone’s
1 To dear Sundar Pichai, Satya Nadella and Mark: this section and actually the whole letter was motivated by an incident with an Apple iPhone
Page 6
software behaviour for devices bound to a locked account.

device services, among these backup storage: For an Apple device there may be the option for a local iTunes backup. People can install third-party apps like Drop-Box or even Google Drive to save files manually outside Apple's walled garden. However, for the automated device backup of the full device (including app data) there only appears to be iCloud. There is no official iCloud API or option in iOS to replace iCloud as backup storage.
Introduction of clear and distinct rules that mandate multiple safety nets:

a) The option to export a cryptographic key to remove the device-binding b) The additional option to set a separate user password to remove the device-binding at any time, independent of the status of the 'identity and account services' online account. c) Rules for reinstatement of accounts / removal of device binding / activation locks, especially when email and phone number are valid and accessible

Regulation that will allow third-party companies to offer backup storage (where backup may still be encrypted by a key held by e.g. Apple and the user, providing the same safety as if the data were residing on Apple servers).
Best wishes,
Le gach dea-ghuí,
Monsieur M.
Page 7
